elements.sent-2411-001: Numerical reasoning is pivotal in various artificial intelligence applications, such as natural language processing and recommender systems, where it involves using entities, relations, and attribute values (e.g., weight, length) to infer new factual relations (e.g., the Nile is longer than the Amazon).
elements.sent-2411-002: However, existing approaches encounter two critical challenges in modeling: (1) semantic relevance—the challenge of insufficiently capturing the necessary contextual interactions among entities, relations, and numerical attributes, often resulting in suboptimal inference; and (2) semantic ambiguity—the difficulty in accurately distinguishing ordinal relationships during numerical reasoning, which compromises the generation of high-quality samples and limits the effectiveness of contrastive learning.
elements.sent-2411-003: To address these challenges, we propose the novel Knowledge-Aware Attributes Embedding model (KAAE) for knowledge graph embeddings in numerical reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-004: Specifically, to overcome the challenge of semantic relevance, we introduce a Mixture-of-Experts-Knowledge-Aware (MoEKA) Encoder, designed to integrate the semantics of entities, relations, and numerical attributes into a joint semantic space.
elements.sent-2411-005: To tackle semantic ambiguity, we implement a new ordinal knowledge contrastive learning (OKCL) strategy that generates high-quality ordinal samples from the original data with the aid of ordinal relations, capturing fine-grained semantic nuances essential for accurate numerical reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-006: Experiments on three public benchmark datasets demonstrate the superior performance of KAAE across various attribute value distributions.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[scale=0.18]{KAAE-NBA_KG.png} \caption{An example of KG-based numerical reasoning. Dashed lines indicate facts that are not relevant to the query, solid lines and underlined attributes indicate relevant facts.} \label{NBA-KG}
elements.sent-2411-007: Knowledge Graphs (KGs) serve as structured representations of real-world facts and knowledge, playing a pivotal role in powering a wide array of downstream applications, such as recommender systems (RS) \cite{DBLP:conf/mm/CaoSW0WY22,DBLP:conf/mm/LiXJCH20,DBLP:conf/mm/HuangFQSLX19 , natural language processing (NLP) \cite{DBLP:conf/mm/Guo0ZM0WX23,DBLP:conf/mm/LuDYY23,DBLP:conf/mm/SunYLLLS23 and multimodal tasks\cite{DBLP:conf/mm/WangMCML023,DBLP:conf/mm/LiQZ0TXT23,DBLP:conf/mm/NiXJCCH23 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-008: Among them, numerical reasoning within KGs emerges as a crucial capability, aimed at identifying and inferring missing numerical relations or hierarchical orders among entities.
elements.sent-2411-009: Existing methods typically integrate additional attributes of entities into traditional knowledge graph embedding (KGE) models, leveraging entities with rich attribute information for numerical reasoning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-010: Others embed numerical entities directly into a vector space or use Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) to enhance KGE performance.
elements.sent-2411-011: However, these approaches often face critical limitations:.
elements.sent-2411-012: Semantic relevance: Most current models fail to capture the intricate interactions among entities, relations, and numerical attributes sufficiently.
elements.sent-2411-013: This deficiency results in suboptimal inference outcomes, as these models do not account for how the significance of numeric attributes changes depending on the relational context.
elements.sent-2411-014: For example, in addressing the query (LeBron James, Taller\_Than, ?), the height attribute is crucial, whereas for (LeBron James, Younger\_Than, ?), age becomes relevant.
elements.sent-2411-015: This selective relevance is often overlooked, leading to the underutilization of semantic details inherent in the numeric attributes, as depicted in Figure \ref{NBA-KG .}
elements.sent-2411-016: Semantic ambiguity: Existing models struggle with accurately distinguishing ordinal relationships between numerical values, which are vital for high-quality inference.
elements.sent-2411-017: Numerical values close in magnitude (e.g., 183cm and 184cm) often carry similar semantic meanings but are prone to erroneous ordinal interpretations under current systems.
elements.sent-2411-018: Such ambiguities can significantly impair the model’s ability to perform effective numerical reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-019: Given these considerations, we pose the following question: Can we develop a novel KGE model that can conduct knowledge-aware attributes learning and exploit latent numerical information within a unified framework? To address these challenges, we introduce a new model termed KAAE (Knowledge-Aware Attributes Embedding), crafted for numerical reasoning via knowledge graphs.
elements.sent-2411-020: Specifically, to tackle (C1), we utilize the Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) framework \cite{DBLP:conf/cikm/BianPZWWW23 to integrate semantic-guided knowledge experts, constructing expert networks for entities across various relational contexts.}
elements.sent-2411-021: Additionally, we implement a joint interaction framework that leverages appropriate contexts and captures the intricate connections between entity-relation pairs and attributes within a unified vector space.
elements.sent-2411-022: Employing this knowledge-aware mechanism, our model discerns the significance and relevance of each attribute, effectively synthesizing the latent semantics among them.
elements.sent-2411-023: To address (C2), diverging from prior work that primarily focuses on positive and negative samples, we introduce a novel ordinal knowledge contrastive learning method to generate high-quality ordinal samples.
elements.sent-2411-024: We optimize these ordinal samples based on cosine similarity, selecting the top-k samples with the highest similarity to enhance the learning of ordinal relations.
elements.sent-2411-025: This approach enables the model to capture the continuous features of numerical attributes and discern the subtle differences in boundary values, thus enhancing numerical reasoning.
elements.sent-2411-026: Furthermore, we provide theoretical guarantees for the effectiveness of our model.
elements.sent-2411-027: Extensive experiments show our model can achieve the state-of-the -art performance.
elements.sent-2411-028: In summary, our contributions are three-fold:.
elements.sent-2411-029: To conduct knowledge-aware attribute learning, we introduce a novel approach, KAAE, from semantic interaction and perceptual perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-030: To our knowledge, this is the first model to address semantic relevance by integrating entity relations and numerical attributes within a unified framework for numerical reasoning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-031: To capture the fine-grained semantics of continuous features for numerical attributes, we propose a novel ordinal knowledge contrastive learning method that generates high-quality ordinal samples.
elements.sent-2411-032: This approach enhances the model's ability to perform nuanced numerical reasoning by exploiting ordinal relationships within the data.
elements.sent-2411-033: Extensive experiments show that KAAE achieves state-of-the-art performance on three real-world datasets, and demonstrate its robustness, reasonability, and interpretability.

elements.sent-2411-049: However, these methods do not consider the joint information among entities, attributes, and relations.
elements.sent-2411-050: On the contrary, our KAAE model can exploit joint complex interactions among entities, relations and numerical attributes to fully mine the semantic information useful for numerical reasoning tasks.. Contrastive Learning.
elements.sent-2411-051: Contrastive learning (CL) is an effective self-supervised learning method by pulling semantically close neighbors together while pushing non-neighbors away, and some models have attempted to utilize contrastive learning to improve representations of entities/graphs.
elements.sent-2411-052: SimGCL\cite{DBLP:conf/sigir/YuY00CN22 adds uniform noise to the entity representation, which is an expansion-free CL method.}
elements.sent-2411-053: HeCO\cite{DBLP:conf/kdd/WangLHS21 employs contrast learning in network schema and meta-path, capturing both local and global features of entities.}
elements.sent-2411-054: SLiCE\cite{DBLP:conf/www/WangAHCR21 makes use of mutual attraction between closed nodes to learn subgraph representations.}
elements.sent-2411-055: RAKGE\cite{DBLP:conf/kdd/KimKKPJP23 interacts with training samples with head entities to generate samples.}
elements.sent-2411-056: However, none of the above methods can guarantee effectiveness in numerical reasoning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-057: To improve contrast learning, we optimize the sampling process with ordinal relations in KGs and generate high-quality ordinal positive/negative samples based on the technique of similarity sampling, thus achieving significant performance improvements.
elements.sent-2411-058: In this section, we present a new KG-based numerical reasoning framework called KAAE from the semantic joint interaction perspective.
elements.sent-2411-059: Specifically, there are two main components: Mixture-of-Experts-Knowledge-Aware Encoder (MoEKA-Encoder) and ordinal knowledge contrastive learning (OKCL) strategy.

elements.sent-2411-122: KAAE consists of three components, the MoEKA encoder, a process of contrastive learning, and the score function.
elements.sent-2411-123: In terms of MoEKA Encoder, the time complexity of processing every triple is $\mathcal{O (\vert\mathcal{M}\vert)$.}
elements.sent-2411-124: The time complexity of the numerical value embedding layer is $\mathcal{O (\vert\mathcal{M}\vert)$ because we transform each attribute field into an embedding vector.}
elements.sent-2411-125: In the joint perceptual attention layer, the query is the target relation and the number of keys and values corresponds to the number of attribute fields, so the time complexity remains $\mathcal{O (\vert\mathcal{M}\vert)$.}
elements.sent-2411-126: The gating layer of the MoEKA Encoder is not involved in the calculation of time complexity.
elements.sent-2411-127: During the process of contrastive learning, for every given triple(h,r,t), the time complexity is proportional to the number of positive samples $|\mathcal{P [h,r]|$ and the number of negative samples $\vert\mathcal{N [h,r]\vert$.}}
elements.sent-2411-128: The time complexity is $\mathcal{O (\vert\mathcal{M}\vert \cdot rel\_{ num)$, where rel\_{num denotes the number of relations per entity.}}}
elements.sent-2411-129: The TransE score function involves only addition and subtraction operations, so it does not change the time complexity.

elements.sent-2411-156: To address these challenges, we introduce a new model termed KAAE (Knowledge-Aware Attributes Embedding), crafted for numerical reasoning via knowledge graphs.
elements.sent-2411-157: Specifically, to tackle semantic relevance, we utilize the Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) framework \cite{DBLP:conf/cikm/BianPZWWW23 to integrate semantic-guided knowledge experts, constructing expert networks for entities across various relational contexts.}
elements.sent-2411-158: To address semantic ambiguity, diverging from prior work that primarily focuses on positive and negative samples, we introduce a novel ordinal knowledge contrastive learning method to generate high-quality ordinal samples.
elements.sent-2411-159: Extensive experiments substantiate the effectiveness of our model.
elements.sent-2411-160: To validate the effectiveness of our ordinal knowledge contrastive learning method, we compared KAAE with those self-supervised models.
elements.sent-2411-161: MRR is improved from 0.331 for BiGI and 0.573 for RAKGE to 0.680 on Spotify dataset, we can deduce KAAE learn richer representations than graph-based approaches including BiGI, SLiCE, and SimGCL by considering extra numerical attributes of entities.
elements.sent-2411-162: Compared to RAKGE, KAAE focuses on generating and extracting hard high-quality ordinal samples to distinguish boundary numerical values and magnitudes accurately, which makes contrastive learning more effective in numerical reasoning tasks.
elements.sent-2411-163: Besides, while HAKE, TuckER, and ConvE often outperform TransE and DistMutl models, Table \ref{Table2 suggests otherwise.}
elements.sent-2411-164: We speculate that this is because numerical reasoning is a one-to-one task in most cases, and is better suited to TransE and DistMult.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Training KAAE} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE Input: Training set $\mathcal{T} = \mathcal{G} \cup \mathcal{G}^{-}$; Positive sample set for $\mathcal{P}[e,r] = \{i|(e,r,i) \in \mathcal{G} \}$; Negative sample set for $\mathcal{N}[e,r] = \{j|(j,r,e) \in \mathcal{G} \}$; Initialized \textit{MoEKA\_{}Encoder}; Comparison relation set $\mathcal{R}_{Comparison}$; Coefficient for contrastive loss $\lambda$; \# of epochs for training epochs \FOR{ 1,2,$\cdots$,epochs} \STATE Initialize loss L = 0 \FOR{each (h, r, t) $\in \mathcal{T}$} \STATE Acquire the head embedding $\bm{e}_{joint,h}^{att}$ = \textit{MoEKA\_{}Encoder(h, r)} \IF{r $\in \mathcal{R}_{Comparison}$ and (h, r, t) $\in \mathcal{G}$} \STATE Get $\mathcal{E}^{+}$ = \{\textit{MoEKA\_{}Encoder(p, r)} | p $ \in \mathcal{P}[h,r]$\} \STATE Get $\mathcal{E}^{-}$ = \{\textit{MoEKA\_{}Encoder(n, r)} | n $ \in \mathcal{N}[h,r]$\} \STATE Generate a high-quality blending positive $\bm{e}_{mix}^{t+}$ with $\mathcal{E}^{+}$ and $\bm{e}_{joint,h}^{att}$ via Eqs. (\ref{EQ15}),(\ref{sample positive}) \STATE Generate a high-quality blending negative $\bm{e}_{mix}^{t-}$ with $\mathcal{E}^{-}$ and $\bm{e}_{joint,h}^{att}$ via Eqs. (\ref{EQ15}),(\ref{sample negative}) \STATE $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}$ + $\lambda \mathcal{L}_{CL}$,($\mathcal{L}_{CL}$ via Eq.(\ref{EQ20})) \ENDIF \STATE $\mathcal{L} = \mathcal{L}$ + $\mathcal{L}_{BCE}$,($\mathcal{L}_{BCE}$ via Eq.(\ref{EQ22})) \ENDFOR \STATE Update \ENDFOR \end{algorithmic} \label{Train}
